There are also instances where no signs are detected, yet changes still occur, indicating a strong fetus.[99p]
    The "Treasured Mirror" states that the first transformation belongs to the Liver Wood, the second to the Lung Metal, the third to the Heart Fire, the fourth to the Spleen Earth, and the fifth to the Kidney Water. The five elements generate and constrain each other, hence there are transformations of evaporation. The Qian family claims that the first transformation associated with Kidney Water represents Heaven generating Water, thus there is a sequence of generation. Both views are compatible. Generally, there are also transformations that do not follow a sequence, such as in the case of cold damage not adhering to the order of the meridians; it depends on which organ shows signs and then adjusts accordingly for the best outcome.

    For instance, if there is evaporation in the Liver, the eyes may become dim and slightly red; if in the Lung, there may be sneezing, coughing, and raised hair; if in the Spleen, it may cause vomiting of milk and diarrhea; if in the Heart, there could be slight panic and intense heat; and if in the Kidney, it could lead to a cold buttocks and hot ears. The signs of the five organs and six bowels can be observed, and one should harmonize them with intention without rigidly adhering to rules and hastily seeking a cure. However, there is naturally a principle behind this transformation of evaporation; for lighter cases, medication is unnecessary, and they will heal spontaneously after a period; for more severe cases that do not heal after a certain time, one should adjust based on the signs, focusing on the center of the issue.
    　　　
    Verification and Treatment Guidelines【Ming - Wang Kentang】[100p]
    Unable to Cry at Birth

    The "Water Mirror Formula" states: When a baby is born and cannot cry due to the fetal wind, one should take small leaves of the gardenia, crush them to extract juice, and mix it with powdered bear bile, then administer it carefully to avoid tilting the baby.

    Not Urinating

    When a newborn does not urinate, it is often due to the mother indulging in excessive eating during pregnancy, causing heat and poison to enter the fetus, resulting in the baby absorbing this contaminated blood. This leads to swelling around the navel. If one notices a bluish-black discoloration around the navel and a contracted mouth, it is beyond help; if there is no bluish-black discoloration and the baby is not nursing, it is advisable to give a scallion and milk soup.

    Not Defecating

    Commonly known as "locked belly," this condition arises from excess heat during gestation, causing heat and poison to accumulate, blocking the anus and preventing bowel movements. If there is no passage by the third day, urgently have the mother rinse her mouth with warm water and apply suction to the baby's back and front chest, as well as the soles of the feet and around the navel, several times (about four or five). Additionally, dissolve half a penny of light powder with a small amount of honey in warm water and administer small doses repeatedly to facilitate bowel movements. If there is still no passage, it indicates a blockage in the anus, and an instrument should be used to unblock it. A gold hairpin is preferable, followed by a jade hairpin. It should be inserted about two inches. Use a Su He Xiang pill inserted into the anus to induce a bowel movement. If the abdomen is swollen and the baby cannot nurse, moans may occur, leading to a grim prognosis by the seventh day.

    Tian’s treatment method: First, use a hard scallion needle inserted into the anus; if there is no defecation, follow up with cow bile powder and administer a vermilion pill, and improvement should be observed shortly.[101p]
    Constipation

    On the first day of a child's life, if the abdomen is bloated and they cannot pass stool or urine, one should apply suction to seven areas: the front chest, back, hands, feet, and below the navel as before, using a reddish color as a guide; in a moment, passage will occur.

            Abscess

    After the child has been born for six or seven days, when blood and energy have congealed into flesh, the mouth, tongue, throat, and cheeks should be clear. If there is something in the throat or on the tongue, resembling a water-filled reed, or if there is a raised swelling, one may use cotton to wrap a long needle, leaving the sharp edge about the size of a grain of millet to prick and drain it, allowing the energy to escape, removing blue, yellow, and red blood essence. After one prick, allow it to rest for a day; if it has not dissipated, prick it again the next day—no more than three pricks should be needed for complete resolution. If small remnants do not go away after three pricks, they will naturally dissipate. If this appears under the tongue, it is called a "heavy tongue"; if it is in the cheeks or on the hard palate, it is called a "heavy palate"; if on the gums, it is called a "heavy gum." All refer to the draining of blood essence. After pricking, wash with salt and promptly apply holy powder or a powdered remedy for cleansing.[102p]
    Mouth Worms

    From "Wa tai": If a child is breastfeeding unsteadily and has had a strong fever for seven days, with a red complexion and yellow nostrils, fear of mouth worms arises. These worms resemble snails or yellow-headed white clams. Mix half a dose of bamboo sap with a bit of beef bile and administer it for treatment; also, by wiping the mouth with pig meat, the worms may come out.

    Wind Stoppage

    Chao Shi states: When a child suddenly has a lump in the mouth, the tongue feels like millet and is unable to suckle, this is called wind stoppage. It results from heat in the womb affecting the internal organs, particularly the heart. From "Sheng hui": Newborns must be cautious of three diseases — mouth worms, wind stoppage, and umbilical wind. All are urgent conditions, especially wind stoppage, which might require a month or more to recover. If the jaw is tight, sucking is unsteady, crying becomes softer, and there’s drooling, people may think it’s due to issues with heat and cold, but they do not realize that the problem lies in the throat and tongue, often resulting in severe conditions. Good clinicians only succeed in treatment three to four times out of ten. If you take protective measures in advance, this condition can be avoided. If there are symptoms present, urgently check the child’s palate for spots; gently scrape them with a fingernail, then administer remedies such as Dingming San or Chen Sha Quan Xie San.[103p]
    If the mouth is locked shut and medicine is ineffective, take fresh Chinese star anise, peel and remove the core, grind it into a very fine powder, mix it with a small amount of dragon's brain incense, and use a finger to dab fresh ginger juice onto the root of the large tooth. It will open up immediately. Although the conditions of wind in the navel, locked mouth, and locked wind differ, their source of illness is the same: a buildup of internal energy that is obstructed and unable to flow freely. It is advisable to take a decoction of fermented soybean juice and consume it to expel the fetal toxins. The "Thousand Golds" states that if a newborn child is strong but shows any minor ailment, it must be treated; if not treated in time, it can lead to serious illness that is difficult to cure. The Zisha pill should be given in measured doses.

    Master Mao states: Within the first one hundred and twenty days of a child's life, if they are struck by wind and their mouth is locked, it is due to the mother suffering from an illness during pregnancy, thus the child receives toxic energy. At birth, the child's blood and qi are not yet balanced, and they are affected by wind evil. Treatment: first give the Duoming powder to induce vomiting of wind phlegm, then use the Xingspi powder and Harmonizing Qi powder together, followed by the Xiongniu powder with Zhu Shagao for continual use to achieve recovery. If the hand is clenched into a fist and the mouth cannot be opened, it can be fatal.[104p]
    Mouth Contraction

    The external signs show a strong tongue and blue lips; the mouth is contracted, and the face is yellow-red, accompanied by labored breathing. The infant is unable to cry and has difficulty nursing. If there is white foam coming from the mouth and the limbs are cold, it is beyond treatment. If the abdomen is swollen with visible veins, and there is pain caused by internal qi stagnation, this is due to a blockage in the intestines and stomach. The treatment emphasizes promoting smoothness and flow, and cinnabar paste is effective. Within the first month, the condition should be avoided, particularly during the first seven days. Zhang Huan states that the infant's fetal qi is accompanied by heat, often transmitted from the mother. If the infant is exposed to cold after bathing, or if wrapped improperly, it may lead to an inability to cry and difficulties in breastfeeding, known as the condition of the mouth not opening. This is particularly serious within the first seven days and requires urgent dispersal of the wind.[105p]
    Navel Wind

    The "Thousand Gold" mentions three conditions: Navel Wind, Navel Sores, and Navel Dampness, all resulting from wind and dampness injuries after the cutting of the umbilical cord. When wind enters the navel, it causes swelling and abdominal distension, impeding the limbs and leading to excessive crying and inability to nurse; in severe cases, it causes convulsions known as Navel Wind. If the swelling and dampness persist for a long time and do not dry, this is termed Navel Dampness. When wind and dampness clash, sores develop in the navel, which do not heal for a long time, leading to Navel Sores. If any of these persist, they can enter the meridians and often transform into epilepsy. If epilepsy develops, it requires treatment for epilepsy.

    According to the Zeng family's method: First, administer Jiafeng ointment; then use Wuling powder combined with Kuanqi drink, mixed with ginger juice, scallion whites, and a decoction of light red dates to resolve wind phlegm; also employ one-character gold, boiled with sweet osmanthus soup or peppermint soup, rub inside the mouth for quick relief if the symptoms are mild. If the constitution is strong, with fever, phlegm, excitement, and convulsions, use black and white drinks, warm honey decoction on an empty stomach, and allow slight diarrhea resembling tea's brown color in two to three lines, then administer Bai Shao Yao decoction, and take with ginger and jujube decoction, consistently using this method is also effective. If the navel is protruding, the abdomen tight, with slight bluish discoloration, and the mouth slightly open, this indicates excessive liver wind constraining the spleen's ability to function, which is unsuitable for treatment. In all such cases, there is almost no remedy, and even if temporary relief is achieved, it is not conducive to longevity.[106p]
    Navel Protrusion

    Zeng noted: The condition of navel protrusion is not the same as navel wind. This occurs when, after birth, the navel is not tightly bound during bathing, allowing dirty water to enter. Within ten days postpartum, the navel may suddenly appear raised and light as if blown up, with a faint sound when touched, and it may sometimes cause the baby to startle and cry. The treatment involves using white peony root decoction with job's tears, prepared by boiling in water and taken warm on an empty stomach; additionally, an external application should be used to help it naturally settle. Navel wind, if occurring within the first hundred days, is often left untreated.[107p]
    Navel Dampness

    If an infant's navel is swollen and damp for a long time without healing, danger arises after a hundred days; urgent treatment is required.

    Navel Sores

    Cao Shi said: When bathing, water may enter the navel, or urine may dampen the bandage, causing the navel to become wet, swollen, and become sore. This can also occur if it is attacked by wind evil, which enters the meridians, leading to convulsions. If the navel remains swollen and damp for too long, it will result in spasms.

    Fetal Shock

    If a child has a strong fever, vomits, is restless, their hands and feet twitch, the body is rigid, and their eyes are rolled back, it indicates fetal wind shock. Fetal shock occurs when the pregnant woman does not follow proper care, indulges in drinking and desires, experiences anger and fright, or has physical contact that affects the fetus; the fetus inevitably feels it. If external wind evil injures the fetus, the child is born sick. Symptoms include high fever in the first month, rolling eyes, clenching fists, mouth closing and grinding teeth, body stiffness, excessive salivation, vomiting, convulsions, crying, retraction of the cheeks, a red face or a pale blue complexion with eyes closed. For instances of fetal wind, with eyes closed and mouth tight, quickly take pig milk, finely grind a small amount of cow bile and musk, mix and apply to the mouth, and also take the Daohong San to purge the child's liver, and that will cure them. The verses from the "Shibi Jing" say: "One month before birth, when the mother is startled, the child suffers. When crying with a stiff back, the mouth is tight and the face turns blue, with fists clenching."[108p]
    Eyes closed and teeth clenched, tendons and veins tense. When receiving shock, if the yin energy is weak, the fetus is prone to fright and miscarriage, leading to significant flesh loss; if the yang energy is weak, the fetus is less likely to be frightened and miscarried, yet the limbs are thin and the flesh emaciated, all signs of not reaching the full span of life. If the sun and moon are full, and the shock causes mouth twitches, facial swelling, nasal congestion, and silence from the mouth, do not treat for shock; with slight sweating, treat the shock by adjusting the energy. The wet nurse should take energy-regulating medicine, and the child should be applied with a soothing paste.

    When called a thousand times, the eyes do not open. Withdraw wind and phlegm for treatment; once the phlegm is gone, the evil of shock will not return. If untreated, the eyes will be closed; treat the shock first to prevent vomiting and diarrhea. In autumn and summer, wind to the spleen must be addressed, but upon first appearance do not treat for spleen wind, for fear that sweating will not lead to other conditions. First use Phoenix Essence and Rhinoceros Horn Pills, then Vermillion Silver Pills.

    Dr. Tang states: to treat fetal convulsions and winds, one may take entire scorpion heads and tails, wrapping them in fresh mint leaves and binding them with hemp thread, then roasting them until dry and grinding to a powder; separately grind a little fresh Vermillion and musk, and decoct them in a mixture of Ophiopogon soup for ingestion.

    Fetal convulsions

    Dr. Zeng states: fetal convulsions occur due to shock to the abdomen before delivery, or the mother’s excessive consumption of sour and salty foods, or being affected by emotional turmoil, which injures the fetal energy. This results in symptoms within the first hundred days post-birth: feelings of unease, a slightly yellow face, reversed breathing with phlegm, upward gaze, body rigidity, and inability to cry. First, use Ginseng and Perilla Drink to harmonize, and then administer Non-shock Pills or Amber Dragon-hugging Pills intermittently. If mild, it can be cured; if severe, recovery is difficult.[109p]
    Fetal Heat

    When a child is in the womb, the mother often experiences anxiety and restlessness, which may be due to consuming hot or toxic foods. After birth, within a few days, the child may present with symptoms such as excessive phlegm, urgent breathing, closed eyes with redness, swollen eyelids, lethargy, yawning frequently, a hoarse voice, a hot body, reddish urine, constipation, and intermittent agitation. This is due to heat accumulated in the womb or improper use of warming agents, leading to internal heat that affects the fetal energy, thus causing these symptoms. If not treated for an extended period, it can lead to conditions such as thrush, heavy tongue, wooden tongue, and red-purple tumors.

    First, the mother should use a herbal formula to disperse the heat, which should be boiled and consumed to be transferred into the milk, allowing the child to drink it to alleviate anxiety and internal heat. Then, the eyes should be washed gently with a warm herbal mixture; once the eyes open, the mother should consume formulas made from rehmannia, turmeric, and other remedies like burdock soup and angelica powder. For any fetal illnesses, one should not seek immediate results. It is important to allow the nursing mother to take the medicine so that it can gradually pass into the milk and release the child from symptoms, ensuring a safe approach. If one uses cooling medicines to attack these symptoms immediately, it may lead to other ailments. The nursing mother should also avoid spicy foods and alcohol to foster recovery and avoid relapse.

    Fetal Cold[110p]
    When an infant is born, within the first hundred days, if they feel a cold sensation in their mouth, experience abdominal pain, and exhibit a shivering body, often accompanied by trembling, curled legs and clenched fists, crying incessantly day and night, or if their mouth is unable to open, this condition is referred to as "cold from the womb." This condition may stem from abdominal pain during pregnancy, which leads to a difficult birth. The classics state that "cold from the womb often entails abdominal pain."

    There are also cases where the mother has cravings for sweet, fatty, cold, or raw foods, or where the fetus is affected by external cold, heat, or dampness, and is treated with cooling medicines that injure the fetal energy, leading to excessive drowsiness after birth, along with occasional vomiting or white diarrhea. If not treated early, this can develop into prolonged fright or spleen disorders. In such cases, it is advisable to administer a harmonizing drink or a Danggui powder mixture, brewed with ginger. This should induce mild discharge, allow energy to spread evenly, and stabilize and soothe the spirit, alleviating pain and allowing limbs to stretch comfortably. Following that, use a Ginseng, Lychee, and Atractylodes mixture to nourish the stomach energy and a Bai Shao decoction to dispel cold and dampness. Wet nurses should regulate their consumption of cold foods and drinks to facilitate healing.

    Another condition presents with cold extremities, slightly cyanotic lips and face, sweating on the forehead, lack of interest in milk or food, and increased crying at night, resembling the previous symptoms but lacking cold sensations and shivering, known as "internal cold." This ailment is typically more severe at night and lighter during the day, accompanied by abdominal pain, bowel sounds, and watery diarrhea, with occasional resistance to diarrhea. This condition can also occur within the first hundred days and results from prolonged exposure to cold air during birth, or if cool water is used to wash the infant, or if the umbilical cord is cut too short or tied too tightly, leading to cold injury. In such cases, it is advisable to use Bai Shao decoction and a harmonizing drink, adding salt-fried fennel anduro within the concoction, boiled with ginger for consumption, and the wet nurse should take it as well.[111p]
    Zhang Huan said: When a baby is born, the eyes must be washed to ensure they are clean; if not, impure fluids can seep into the corners of the eyes, causing the face to become red and sore, leading to a condition called "胎赤" (fetal red) that does not heal as they grow up. It is advisable to use the "二金散" (Two Gold Powder).

    Zeng Shi noted: If a child born in the pure Yang period has both eyes suddenly red and exhibits symptoms like blackish, itchy spots, this is due to the mother having been affected by wind-heat during pregnancy, which transmits to the heart and liver of the child. First, use "百解散" (Hundred Relief Powder) combined with "当归散" (Angelica Powder), boiled with ginger water and taken as a decoction; then use "导赤散" (Red Guiding Powder) alongside "牛蒡汤" (Burdock Soup) mixed with "黄连" (Coptis), "木贼" (Equisetum), and "蝉壳" (Cicada Shell), boiled for consumption for effective results.

    There are also cases of difficult labor, where the fetal energy is quite thick and turns sideways, causing blood pressure on the child's head, leading to bleeding that can splash into the eyes, potentially obscuring the pupil; in lighter cases, it results in swelling and redness of the outer parts. If cooling medicines are administered, they will inevitably chill the internal organs, so it's better to use "生地黄汤" (Raw Rehmannia Decoction) as a treatment.

    胎黄 (Fetal Yellow)

    When a child is born with a body and face all appearing yellow, resembling a golden hue, the body feels hot, constipation occurs, and urine resembles the juice of gardenia, along with a lack of desire for milk and continuous crying, these are signs of "胎黄" (fetal jaundice), caused by the wet heat transmitted to the fetus from the wet nurse. In this case, both mother and child should take "地黄汤" (Rehmannia Decoction) and "地黄饮子" (Rehmannia Drink).

    If the child is born and appears slightly yellow after a hundred days or half a month without prior illness, this indicates stomach heat; if the yellow appears from birth, it signifies fetal jaundice. The classics state that all jaundice is heat-related, with deep yellow indicating this condition is treated with "犀角散" (Rhinoceros Horn Powder). If the yellow is light and mixed with white, it indicates stomach deficiency, treated with "白术散" (Atractylodes Powder).[112p]

    Coughing in Infants

    The Zeng family states: If a child coughs and has phlegm blockage within a hundred days of birth, and sleeps restlessly, it may be due to postnatal wind invasion. However, one should not excessively use dispersing agents. First, a couple of doses to resolve the exterior should be administered, followed by the use of Fritillaria Decoction and Xingxing Powder for treatment.

    Crying

    If a child has startled cries, night crying, or squirming cries: Startled cries are caused by wind evil invading the heart, generating heat in the internal organs, which makes the spirit unstable and sleep uneasy. Night crying occurs due to cold in the internal organs; at night, the Yin is strong, and the strong Yin interacts, causing more pain than during the day, thus leading to night crying. Some believe it could also be due to violating taboos, which causes the child to cry at night; one could perform rituals to discern this. As for squirming cries, they arise from severe abdominal pain, causing the child to squirm and cry out as their energy rises. Additionally, there are cases of crying due to fetal cold, suggesting that the child had already been ill in the womb. These children typically show signs of weak and cold digestive organs, difficulty in breastfeeding, abdominal distension, and diarrhea, often presenting with a pale blue-white complexion and crying intermittently. Furthermore, sorrowful cries indicate the voice of the lungs, while tears are the fluid of the liver.[113p]
    If the six meridians are tense, first soak the body in warm water to induce sweating, and then use cool herbs like Liangge San to clear internal heat. This is the method prescribed by Zhang Zihe. If this is due to the anger of a wet nurse, resulting in residual heat in the liver, which causes fiery conflict with the lung's metal element, thus leading to weeping with sound, one should use the Six Gentlemen Decoction with Chaihu and Shan Zhi to tonify the spleen and clear the liver, and use the Six-Flavor Pill to strengthen water to nourish wood.

    There may be cases caused by fright leading to excessive use of wind-dispelling and blood-drying medicines, or due to vomiting and diarrhea resulting in the loss of body fluids, or from a hereditary insufficiency of kidney yin that cannot nourish the liver. Each case should be analyzed carefully. If a child suddenly cries out loudly, Danxi stated it is a sign of imminent death. This is due to insufficient kidney yin, causing empty fire to rise. The Six-Flavor Pill often has cases of survival. One should also consider symptoms like night crying, visitor's interference, fright-induced crying, heavy tongue, mouth sores, Tian Diao, and Nei Diao, among others.[114p]
    The Five Weaknesses

    The Dai family discusses the "Five Weaknesses," known as "fetal weakness," which arises from insufficient paternal essence and diminished maternal blood. Truly, this is wise! From my limited understanding, it can be attributed to various causes: a long duration of cold in the maternal blood, leading to imbalances during pregnancy; conditions where the mother is often ill; a father inclined to lust and excessive drinking, resulting in weak physical constitution; or cases where advanced age leads to later childbirth; or instances where insufficient nourishment during gestation results in the fetus being carried while the mother endures not removing the abortive medicine, thus depleting vital energy. Even if life occurs, it is like plants growing in shallow soil, where few thrive robustly. Also, just as it is rare for trees to thrive when their roots are disturbed.

    Consequently, it follows that infants displaying weakness and inability to withstand cold and heat often grow into adults who are also chronically ill. From the moment of birth, they lack vital essence, suffer from weak muscles and bones, have undernourished flesh, a dull and sluggish appearance, and are quickly invaded by the six excesses. This results in laxity in the head, neck, limbs, and body, which is termed "the Five Weaknesses." Treatment involves using regulating herbal powders and kidney-nourishing pills, gradually nurturing them until they stabilize over time. If medication fails, it results in a person of little value.[115p]
    Introduction to Medicine (Ming Dynasty, Li Zhan)

    Newborns

    Why do newborns need medical attention? They may suffer from heat, cold, or improper weight while in the womb. Coldness leads to a chilled body and frequent diarrhea, with intermittent pains in the intestines. Some may be unable to cry or experience kidney shrinkage. Newborns with kidney shrinkage are those who have been exposed to cold. Use five qian each of sulfur and Wu Zhu Yu, ground into a powder, mixed with garlic juice, and applied on the abdomen; also, burn the seeds of the snake bed plant to create a gentle smoke.

    When there are blisters all over the body or lack of skin, heat from the womb can cause abscesses leading to death after birth. If there are no openings in the passage, it is also unusual. Inability to urinate or defecate may result from not breastfeeding; if accompanied by abdominal distention, it becomes difficult to endure. If a newborn appears red like cinnabar, it may have jaundice; urgent care is needed for oral sores. If the tongue is heavy, the tongue has a woody texture, and the gums are white, these suggest that the mother’s heat has been passed on to the child.[116p]
    Mouth Seizures

    Mouth seizures manifest with rapid breathing, heat and toxins entering the heart and spleen during gestation. There are also cases of cold in the spleen and lungs, where excessive salivation and cold limbs are not manageable.

    Jaw Clenching

    When clenching the jaw, the infant cannot nurse or cry; the internal heat exacerbates the wind causing this condition. These symptoms arise from stagnant internal energy, and expelling phlegm to relieve shock is the best approach. If the nose is blown to induce sneezing, it may also help; seeing this within seven days is quite dangerous.

    Navel Wind and Fetal Wind

    Navel wind is characterized by cold dampness flowing through the navel, causing swelling and abdominal distension with soft limbs. Affected infants may excessively cry, convulse, or exhibit jaw clenching. If there is heat present, recovery is possible. If excessive crying prevents feeding day and night, leading to severe convulsions or jaw clenching, it signifies internal convulsions that cannot be treated. Any signs of bluish-black around the navel or nails indicate death. Classic treatments include Great Expelling Shock Pill; alternatively, nasal blowing techniques can be employed, and sneezing could provide relief. In severe cases, Jinwu Powder or external remedies like Saiming Pill or a pinch of Jinxiaomiao can be used. If the convulsions stabilize slightly, but there is excessive crying and agitation, the Great Warming Shock Pill is recommended. Heat in the chest that causes strain can also lead to navel swelling; treatments include Qianjin Longdan Decoction and Lesser Cooling Shock Pill.

    Fetal wind accompanied by convulsions often results in vomiting, with affected infants showing red at the brow.[117p]
    Fetal Alarm and Night Crying

    When the night is filled with startling cries, often accompanied by phlegm and fever, the child may sweat and have flushed cheeks. The alarming cries during the night, particularly when the moon is out, are due to fright experienced in the womb, and the remedy is pig’s milk cream. Use one qian of amber and one qian of fangfeng, along with five fen of red lead, ground into a powder. Mix this with pig's milk to apply to the child's mouth; alternatively, use life-preserving elixirs or heart-calming pills made with gold leaf. If fright is accompanied by phlegm, use Longyuan Pills; if heat is present, use Cool Fright Pills or Dragon Brain Calming Pills.

    For usual cases of heat-induced night crying, use three pieces of lampwick, powdered and decocted in water, applied to the child’s mouth, and taken with milk as a dose twice daily. Generally, a child with phlegm and heat will cry during the latter part of the night, often sweating while lying on their back, with a flushed face and restless heart. They may have red and painful urination, as well as heat in the mouth and abdomen.

    Crying during the night also hints at possible deficiency and coldness in the lower back, and in severe cases, there may be spasms of the hands and feet.

    For those with weak breath leading to night cries, a formula of Four Noble Herbs can be used, with the addition of yam and hyacinth beans. If heat is present, add Huanglian and bamboo leaves.[118p]
    For those who cry due to blood deficiency, use angelica as a powder mixed with breast milk for administration. For cases of both qi and blood deficiency accompanied by abdominal pain and nighttime crying, use equal parts of astragalus, angelica, red peony, muscone, and licorice, ground into a powder, and apply a small amount to the nipple to allow the baby to suckle it. If there is fetal cold or if clothing and bedding are too cool, leading to internal cold affecting the organs, causing abdominal distension and pain, and the child cries while looking up with their eyes and twitching their hands and feet, this is usually due to the excess of yin at night, resulting in pain from the cold, which can cause excessive agitation, hence the nighttime crying. In such cases, care should be taken with life-preserving formulas; for milder cases, a beneficial yellow powder may be used, with external treatment of roasted wheat bran. Generally, if the child cries while bending at the waist in the middle of the night, has a pale blue face, and feels cold to the touch on the abdomen, it is likely due to cold-induced abdominal pain. If crying occurs due to shock from wind evils, a recipe called Erhuo Powder should be used: two parts of Qianghuo and Duhuo each, and one part each of areca nut, Tianma, ephedra, and licorice, boiled in water. Alternatively, powdered nanchang can be added to honey for external application on the forehead. If pain from consumed milk or food leads to crying, use the Digestive Milk Food Pill.

    If there is continuous crying at night, especially around dusk, it indicates obstruction from foreign evils. If crying occurs from drinking, leading to sores on the tongue and mouth, further treatment is needed.[119p]
    If a child desires to drink milk but cries upon reaching the mouth, with a hot body and forehead, and if the mouth appears without sores, it is likely that the throat and tongue are swollen and painful. One should treat this with ice plum pills and mint decoction. Generally, a newborn who cries frequently within the first month is a good omen. If there is excess heat, toxins, or startling from the fetus that can disperse, and no unusual ailments are present, one must know that frequent bathing can harm the abdomen and lead to umbilical wind; not avoiding contact with unfamiliar people or objects may cause fright, startling cries; excessive milk or food can cause vomiting, diarrhea, or phlegm influx; excessive warmth can lead to sores in the mouth and tongue; while excessive coolness can cause internal cold and qi stagnation. Thus, the method of regulation should be moderate.

    Concerning the changes and steaming:

    Fright causes qi to rise and steaming causes heat, with eight steams and ten changes affecting the creation of qi and blood. A newborn, although its form is complete, has yet to fully develop its organs, qi, and blood, and its emotions, consciousness, spirit, and soul are not yet fully formed. Thus, once the steaming changes are complete, the child begins to learn to speak, supports itself while standing, and is capable of eating, with blood vessels, bones, and sinews all secure. Those with vigorous qi may seem fine without outward signs; those with weak qi may exhibit steaming illnesses.

    If light, there may be slight sweating resembling fright.
    If light, there may be slight fever and sweating, resembling fright, which resolves in five days.
    If severe, there is strong heat, vomiting, and thirst.
    If severe, there is strong heat, a rapid and chaotic pulse, possible vomiting or sweating, restlessness, crying, and thirst, resolving after seven or eight days, resembling typhoid. There may also be cases after steaming where cold pathogens are acquired; for example, steaming in the liver may cause faint red eyes, and steaming in the lungs may cause sneezing and coughing. Treatment should align with the symptoms, and it is important to maintain harmony, with slight sweating or purging.[120p]
    Without sweating but feeling hot, if there is a slight perspiration, it resembles milk being expelled; this should be gently stopped and should not be recklessly treated. It is advisable to administer a calming drink. One and a half qian of white poria, five fen each of ginseng and licorice, and two fen of wspurge. For those who are weak and frail, add one qian of white atractylodes, and cook it in water to consume. Take a dose before and after steaming on the third day, which can prevent many illnesses; it is also advisable within a hundred days. For those who vomit and have diarrhea, and cry excessively without being nourished, use a harmonizing herbal powder. Equal parts of wood incense, cyperus, magnolia bark, ginseng, dried tangerine peel, patchouli, and licorice, cooked with ginger and jujube to consume. For retained milk, use the Purple Frost Pill; for phlegm heat, use the Lingering Wake Powder.

    For heat in the bones and heart disturbance, with continuous crying, use the Chai Hu Decoction. Two fen each of Chai Hu, ginseng, ophiopogon, and licorice, and one fen each of gentian and saposhnikovia, cooked in water to consume. If there is cold without heat, use the Angelica Decoction. Four fen of angelica, and two fen each of wood incense, spicy cinnamon, ginseng, and licorice, cooked with ginger and jujube to consume. If steaming heat is severe, use the Purple Yang Black Powder; for accumulated heat with cold and heat alternating like malaria, use the Pear Juice Drink.

    Five Softnesses and Five Hardnesses

    The five soft conditions are due to a deficiency of constitution, resulting in delayed movement, speech, and the growth of teeth and hair. The five hard conditions refer to rigidity due to wind syndromes, often accompanied by a rigid abdomen and accumulation.[121p]

    Cough

    Coughing in infants within a hundred days is not advisable, as it can damage the stomach and lungs, posing a serious risk. It may occur due to crying excessively, which prevents breastfeeding, or from overeating, leading to obstruction in the chest and stomach, thereby affecting the lungs and causing coughing and hiccups, indicating illness in both the lungs and stomach. If this occurs within a hundred days, it is a bad sign. For cough with fever, if the face is red, use a pill with cinnamon and licorice.

    If the cough is caused by seasonal colds, one should use ginseng and perilla drink or similar mild treatments; if accompanied by severe heat and a red face, it is advisable to take a pill with licorice. For those with deficiency, ejiao powder may be helpful.

    Diseases Returning to Spring [Ming - Gong Tingxian]

    Newborns

    For newborns, if white spots appear on the palate along with the gums, known as "horse teeth," they cannot breastfeed. This is different from thrush; if it worsens, it often leads to death. Urgently, use a needle to bind the point on the head, puncturing the white spots until they bleed. Then use high-quality ink mixed with mint tea, dipping a finger in the oil from a mother's hair to apply the mixture thoroughly in the mouth and palate. Do not offer milk or food, and wait for the child to wake from sleep before providing milk again, reapplying the mixture. Infants should not experience constant changes in heat; changing is abnormal, while heating indicates growth. It is necessary for the five organs to transform healthily, nurturing the six bowels, requiring such changes. Frequent changes nourish the qi and blood, allowing bones and veins to grow, thus leading to lighter future ailments like pox. Regular changes in health should not involve medication.[122p]
    Sometimes, illnesses arise due to a combination of food injury, wind injury, or shock, which can lead to confusion. One must wait a day or two to see if the condition truly manifests; if it is food-related, then one should dispel the food; if it's due to wind, one should promote phlegm; if it’s from shock, one should soothe the spirit. If one hastily administers medicine when the symptoms change abruptly, it leads to complications in the channels, making diagnosis difficult and causing prolonged issues, as the medicine may have reacted improperly.

    In one case, a child within the month exhibited convulsions and nasal congestion due to wind evil. A mixture of six gentleman soup, adding half a stalk of platycodon and a pinch of asarum, was given to both mother and child. Additionally, seven green onions and a slice of fresh ginger were crushed together, placed on paper, and applied heatedly to the Yintang point. After a short while, the nasal congestion and convulsions ceased.

    In another case, a child not yet a month old had convulsions and vomited milk, accompanied by abdominal distension and diarrhea due to milk injuring the spleen and stomach. A modified five-flavor powder was used, adding bulrush, to be taken by the mother. The child was also given a small spoonful, and thus recovered.[123p]
    Discussion on the General Principles of Child Health: Five Qi Theory

    From the time a child is conceived until birth, it inherits the five elements associated with the five organs. Thus, the five Qi give rise to five states, each varying in nature. The "Shengji Jing" states that the elements nurture the unique qualities; the five states each embody different energies. Equilibrium and harmony confer certain attributes; innate characteristics may differ due to maternal energy during pregnancy, experiencing fluctuations of strength and weakness, leading the offspring to exhibit traits of courage and timidity. Moreover, it is stated that if the heart’s Qi is weak, speech may be delayed; if the liver’s Qi is insufficient, movement may be slow; if the spleen’s Qi is weak, the flesh may be thin; and if the kidney’s Qi is weak, cognitive faculties may diminish. All such cases are included here. However, for those whose Qi in the five organs differs, the sages also possess remedies to nourish and restore them.[124p]
    Initial Birth

    For infants suffering from umbilical sores that have not healed, they should not be allowed to overfeed, as excessive fullness can lead to umbilical wind. If the infant is born with a delayed or late severance of the umbilical cord, it can expose them to cold, resulting in internal coldness. Moreover, if the umbilical cord is cut before bathing, water vapor can enter, or exposure to water vapor during bathing may also lead to internal coldness. Symptoms include abdominal cramping, sudden cries, a pale or dark face, and clear urination. One should use powdered Angelica, placing it on cotton, heating it, and applying it to the abdomen and umbilical area repeatedly every day until the crying stops and the umbilicus dries out. Care should be taken not to overheat it, for fear of causing fright.

    If, within the first seven days of birth, the infant suddenly develops a pale face, cannot cry, has tightly pursed lips, cannot suckle, has a bluish mouth, and frothy white saliva, along with cold extremities, this indicates a case of umbilical wind causing lip lock. This may result from improper severing of the umbilical cord or being affected by wind and dampness. If it is mild, the condition may be limited to the skin, resulting in umbilical sores that do not heal; if severe, it can afflict the internal organs, leading to umbilical wind causing lip lock.[125p]
    Just as an adult becomes sensitive to wind due to an injury, resulting in clenched jaws and a mouth that cannot consume food, leading to a stiff body and extremities turning cold—this scenario is quite similar; hence it is called "navel wind and clenched mouth," which is a most severe illness. One should urgently examine the child's mouth, particularly between the upper and lower gums; if there is a white spot about the size of a bean, pinch it with a fingernail from the outside to the inside, ensuring it is surrounded until a little blood appears, which is not a concern. Additionally, at both ends of the white area, apply a pinch accordingly to disrupt the internal and external energy flow, and it is not necessary to directly pierce the nail, for excessive force may harm the child. The "Secrets of Mother and Child" state: apply centipede powder to the pinched area.

    When a child is born, ensure that all openings are kept open and not closed, to avoid clenching and tightening of the mouth. If a child suffers from illness within a year of birth, it is often because, prior to birth, the mother consumed toxic substances, causing the fetus to react, which results in the manifestation of disease after birth. There are also cases where during pregnancy, the mother fails to properly nourish herself, leading to insufficient vitality for the fetus, which gives rise to illness. Thus, the "Sage's Healing Classic" states that illness originating within is concurrent with birth.[126p]
    From birth until seven days, if a child suddenly cannot nurse, has an urgent abdomen and cries excessively, passes dark greenish-black stool, or has mouth sores resembling grains of corn, this is called "mouth closure." This symptom shows tightness in the jaw and mouth closure, similar to a "mouth contraction" condition. In mouth contraction, the face appears blue, and no sound comes from the cries, with white foam expelled from the mouth; in the case of mouth closure, the face appears red, cries more, and does not expel white foam, which is the distinguishing factor. This occurs due to wind and heat injuring the fetus while in the womb. Therefore, the "Holy Treatise" states: wind and heat injure the fetus, leading to mouth closure upon birth.

    Since birth, if the child's complexion is bluish-white without blood color, constantly crying day and night without cease, body arching backward, especially at night, abdominal fullness with inability to nurse, passing bluish-white stool and crying continuously without response to various medicines, this is named "arching and crying." This condition arises from wind and cold injuring the fetus before birth. Therefore, the "Holy Treatise" states: wind and cold injure the fetus, leading to arching and crying upon birth.[127p]
    The "Thousand Gold" states: When an infant has cold from the womb, they experience abdominal pain and cry, often vomiting. Sometimes, there is a feeling in the abdomen as if there is a large yolk of an egg, which when pressed sounds like water but then disappears and returns. If not treated promptly, this can often lead to epilepsy.

    There are two reasons why infants may have difficulty breastfeeding: First, when a child is born, they may be affected by external wind and heat entering through the umbilical cord, flowing into the heart and spleen channels, causing thick tongue and dry lips, making it difficult to latch on to breast milk and thus unable to nurse. Second, if the infant at birth does not properly expel the dirty blood from the mouth, which then swallows and enters the abdomen, it leads to a feeling of fullness in the heart and abdomen, causing shortness of breath and difficulty in nursing.

    It is advisable to administer Five Blessings Detoxification Pills and similar medicines.

    Before birth, while in the mother’s womb, the infant is nourished by the mother’s menstrual blood. At birth, it is necessary to wipe away the bad blood from the mouth and use medicine to thoroughly expel the filthy blood and feces from the infant's abdomen before feeding. If the bad blood in the infant's mouth is not properly cleaned at birth and is swallowed into the abdomen, or if there are remnants of old impurities that have not been expelled, this can lead to a condition known as blood phlegm, also called fetal accumulation.

    In the first hundred days after birth, if the infant develops sores on the head, red and watery eyes, urine that resembles blood, breathlessness, a purplish body, abdominal distension, irritability, and excessive crying without nursing.[128p]
    On Transformation and Steam

    When a child is in the mother’s womb, after ten months of gestation, although the skin, muscles, bones, viscera, and vital energy and blood are all present, they are not yet fully developed. Hence, there is transformation and steam, as the spirit and intellect strengthen the bones and vessels. Transformation refers to change, while steam refers to heat. After each passage of the meridian, the bones, vessels, and vital energy of the child become progressively stronger, and their spirit and temperament become distinct.

    Thus, the "Sacred Treatise" states that infants undergo transformation and steam at birth because their physical bodies are intact but not yet complete, and their spirit has not yet matured. They still rely on the interplay of yin and yang, and on the balance of water and fire, being shaped accordingly—not that transformation is a natural constant dictated by the Way.

    Therefore, a child naturally undergoes transformation every thirty-two days, corresponding to the thirty segments of bones in the fingers (with each of the ten fingers having three segments), plus the thirty-two segments in the palms, and similarly for the feet. The Grand Master of the Warehouse states: Qi, once it enters, nurtures the muscles and bones in a cycle of ten transformations, and this is it. The "Sacred Treatise" also states: transformation pertains to upper qi, and steam refers to the heat of the body.[129p]
    When the qi is elevated, the five organs are transformed and ascend to the upper realm, with the true essence hidden above the lungs; when the body is warm, the blood vessels flourish, and the yang, being external, serves the yin. Therefore, when the transformation reaches completion, the form and qi are fully realized. It is akin to the birth of all things — without the interplay of yin and yang, and the warmth of qi, there would be no flourishing transformation.

    When a child is to undergo transformation, they do not desire food; if they eat, they will immediately vomit. If their eyes are white, it is serious; if their eyes are red and dark, they are slightly closed and do not open. Once the transformation is complete, their vision will naturally become clear. The lighter cases will decline in five days, while the heavier cases will take ten days. Some may decline five days earlier, while others may do so five days later; or the heat will only subside in the tenth day. If the heat is severe and persists through the night without rest, it is vital not to be startled, nor to allow others nearby to speak too much and confuse the situation. It is not advisable to recklessly perform moxibustion, but giving them a small amount of purple pills can effectively stop the heat.

    One may also use Sun Zhenren’s elbow-back black powder or fragrant powder for treatment, but nothing else should be added. If during a season of epidemic febrile diseases and heated conditions, although they resemble the transformation, with symptoms like ear heat, buttock heat, and no white vesicles on the lips, these are signs of other illnesses and should be treated according to their respective symptoms. If there is cold mixed within the transformation, causing a clash between cold and heat, with abdominal pain and incessant crying, applying a hot compress can bring relief.[130p]

    The Transformation of Steam Rhyme

    When observing the illness of an infant, one must first understand the four seasons. There are signs of transformation and steaming, akin to the vigorous drive of warmth. As cold and heat initially arrive, one must carefully manage the balance; when the cycle is incomplete, one should consider the age of the child.

    The spirit and soul are beginning to form, and the sinews and bones are starting to gain strength. Opening the pores and channels, clarifying the wondrous meridians. Utilizing this to align yin and yang, the circulation of blood and vital energy begins to stabilize. Suddenly, one feels the spirit of the infant—a true distinction arises; the nature of transformation and steaming needs to be understood in detail.

    The liver corresponds to the east, associated with wood, and serves as a reflective symbol manifested in the eyes. If excessive heat rises, the eyes may lose their brightness; a pervasive heaviness fills the body, indicating the spirit and soul are insufficient. The number four relates to metal, with two linked to fire. Coughing occurs frequently, beads of sweat accumulate. In just two days, sneezing increases; numerous nights of anxiety can also follow.

    As the marrow grows dense, there is a tendency to repeatedly turn and shift; gradually, the character develops, leading to fear of others while yearning for affection.[131p]
    The spleen’s earth breathes five times, the kidney’s water resonates once. The energy from grains dims and draws forth, while essence and will increase with time. Nourishing milk is sweet, the child sits alone in play; the meridians flow smoothly, with the bones of the hands developed, allowing for crawling. Gradually, the tendons and veins change, the knees and ankles transform. Qi fills and nourishes the limbs, radiating brightness across the face. Standing tall, yet still unsteady, the soft sounds of babbling voices tremble. The hands and feet receive blood, moving and walking elicits pity; the ears and eyes connect with the spirit, giving rise to understanding and wisdom that is envied. This is established through breathing, making the body robust. Two energies: external yang and internal yin, one breath entering through the nose and exiting. When the time comes, naturally the organs are filled; if nourishment is inappropriate, it can lead to severe pathogenic wind. Thus, the hair stands without moisture, milk is seen at the mouth; pearls rise on rosy lips, while cold invades the back and ears. Sometimes there’s rumbling in the intestines leading to slight diarrhea, or startled cries that should not bring joy. The classic states: steaming signifies the solid meat and blood, transforming means changing the essence and spirit to their proper state.

    Initial Birth Song

    In the child’s first month after birth, silence is crucial, as illness can be grave. Exhaling and blowing foam, the fetus was long frightened. The chest sinks and the hands frequently flip; parents lament, as it is hard to ease. In the child’s first month post-birth, anxiety accumulates, transforming into umbilical wind. The mouth resembles that of a fish; destiny must reach its conclusion.[132p]
    Complete Collection of Medical Texts from Ancient and Modern Times, Volume Four Hundred Nine

    Section on Infantile Diseases

    Prescription

    Long Dan Decoction [from "Thousand Gold Prescriptions," same below] is used to treat infants newly born with strong blood and pulse, experiencing cold and heat, stiff limbs, fever, and severe vomiting. If they can start feeding but are not digesting properly, leading to strong heat; or if they experience transformation and steaming issues, along with ghostly influences, and other convulsions—it is suitable for all such cases. Children under the age of ten should all take this. Long Dan Decoction is the primary remedy for newborn infants. For those who grow progressively, follow this as a standard: for a hundred-day-old child, add three coins; for a two-hundred-day-old child, add half a tael; the remainder of the ingredients are adjusted accordingly.

    Ingredients: Long Dan Grass, Diao Teng Skin, Chai Hu, Huang Qin, Jie Geng, Shao Yao, Fu Ling (also known as Fu Shen), and Gan Cao, each six coins; Two beetles, and one tael of Da Huang.

    Prepare by grinding these ten ingredients, using one liter of water, boil down to five cups as the dosage. Administer as follows. The dosage must consider the condition of the child; for weak children, the amount of water should be increased. For infants aged one day to seven days, divide one cup into three doses; for those eight days to fifteen days, divide one and a half cups into three doses; for sixteen to twenty days old, divide two cups into three doses; for twenty to thirty days old, divide three cups into three doses; for thirty to forty days old, use the entire five cups divided into three doses. Stop once the child has a bowel movement; do not administer again.[133p]
    Ziyuan treats children with evaporative heat that doesn't resolve, often accompanied by chills and warmth, where heat persists after sweating. It also addresses cases of phlegm and mass in the abdomen, lack of appetite during breastfeeding, vomiting milk, food cravings, and a sequence of cold followed by heat.

    The ingredients include:

    • Red clay fat, roasted
    • Redstone, calcined and soaked in vinegar seven times, each one liang
    • Thirty seeds of chuanbei (the seed of the poisonous plant) with shells and membrane removed
    • Fifty almonds, with skins and tips removed

    Grind the four ingredients into a fine powder, and separately grind the chuanbei and almonds into a paste. Mix them thoroughly and pound again for two thousand strokes until they unify. If it becomes too hard, incorporate a bit of honey to help with the grinding, then store it in a sealed container for thirty days.

    For children, administer a pill the size of a sesame seed with a little breast milk. After a meal, provide a bit of milk, but do not give too much. By noon, a small amount should be excreted, reducing the heat. If not completely resolved, give another pill the next morning. For infants of a hundred days, use a pill the size of a small bean. Adjust dosage according to the child's needs; in hot summer months, it can evoke rashes effectively, and giving it every two or three weeks is beneficial.

    Ziyuan is versatile in treating conditions and is not harmful to healthy individuals. According to Qian's "Direct Formula," the recipe omits red clay fat and is named Zisha Pills. The "Complete Book of Pediatric Science" mentions its effectiveness in treating abdominal distension, blue veins, and retention of phlegm in children.

    It is crucial to note that a child is born with strong vital energy, so even a slight ailment should be addressed promptly to prevent it from developing into a more serious illness. However, those with inherently weak vital energy should not use this method.[134p]
    Heisan: Treats children's variola during steaming conditions, especially when there is an epidemic warm disease, or those who contract it outside of the steaming time.
    Mahuang and Xingren each half tael, Dahuang six zhū.

    Mix the three ingredients: first, grind Mahuang and Dahuang into a powder, separately grind the Xingren until it becomes pasty, then finely mix them together and grind until thoroughly blended. Store in a sealed container. For a child, administer one bean-sized amount mixed with milk for a month, keeping them warm to induce sweating; be cautious that they do not catch a draft while sweating. For a hundred-day-old child, give a dose the size of a jujube pit, adjusted according to the child's size.

    Baishizhi Powder: Treats children with persistent navel discharge and accompanying redness and swelling.
    Baishizhi: Finely grind, warm slightly, and dust onto the navel sore three to four times a day.

    Puhuang Decoction: Treats children who fall from beds and injure themselves, showing signs of blood stasis, with cold and heat persisting, crying during nursing.
    Puhuang, Maidong, Dahuang, Huangqin each ten zhū, Gancao eight zhū, Mangxiao seven zhū, Huanglian twelve zhū.

    Mix these seven ingredients (pounded) and boil in two sheng of water until reduced to one sheng, then strain, add Mangxiao, and divide into three doses. Monitor the child for weight gain; if there is blood in urine or stool, they will recover. Avoid cold foods.[135p]
    Danggui Pill: Treats cold in children's bodies causing crying, abdominal pain, a black tongue, and green saliva. Also known as Black Pill. Ingredients: 9 taels of Danggui, 9 taels of Wolf poison, half a tael each of Wuyi (or almonds), Xi Xin, Sheng Jiang, and Fu Zi, 10 seeds of Ba Dou, and 7 portions of Chi. Omit one ingredient. Grind these nine ingredients, pound them, and sift them until fine. Grind the Ba Dou and Chi into a paste, mix slightly with the powder, and then combine with honey in a steamer containing five sheng of rice, steaming it until it has been pounded a thousand times. Administer one pearl the size of millet to the child once every day and night. Adjust dosage cautiously. Also treats water phobia.

    Machai Pill: Treats cold in children's bodies causing crying, fright, abdominal distention, lack of appetite, and diarrhea of greenish-yellow stools. One piece of Machai, burned for half a day. Mix with jujube paste into balls the size of walnuts; adults take two pills three times a day. Reduce for children based on abdominal warmth. It’s extremely effective when food is avoided.

    Longgu Pill: Treats children's fright and nighttime crying. Also known as Wu Jing Wan by the Cui family. Ingredients: 6 taels of Longgu, 7 taels each of Oyster (or Peony) and Chuan Da Huang, half a tael of Huang Qin, 2 pieces of cicada, and 5 beans-sized Ox Gall. Grind the six ingredients into a powder, form into pills the size of sesame seeds, and give two pills to the child, adjusting dosage based on the child's size.

    Xiongzhang Powder: Treats children's nighttime crying and ensures peaceful sleep by morning. Ingredients: half a tael each of Xiongzhang, Bai Zhu, and Fang Ji.[136p]
    Here is the translation of the Classical Chinese text:

    "The three ingredients are prepared with milk and given to the child, with the amount determined as needed. Additionally, the child's mother covers the navel with her hand and massages the child's head and back as a form of examination. If the child cannot take the powdered medicine after twenty days, mix it with milk and administer it in the size of a sesame seed ball. For older children who can take the powder, dosage should be adjusted according to their needs.

    For treating infants with nighttime crying, use the ingredient 'Qian Hu' (Fritillaria). Depending on the amount, pound it into a powder, mix it with honey to form balls the size of a large soybean, and administer one ball three times a day, gradually increasing to five or six balls as recovery dictates.

    The 'Xie Qing Wan' (Purge Green Pills), according to Qian's "Zhi Jue," is for treating young children's liver channel excess heat and sudden convulsions. Also called 'Xie Gan Wan' (Purge Liver Pills).

    The ingredients include: Qiang Huo (notopterygium) combined with Ren (for this treatment), and longdan (gentian), which is roasted to supplement liver and gallbladder Qi to quell fright; Da Huang (rhubarb) to eliminate excess heat; Chuan Xiong (ligusticum) that enters the Shao Yin channels of the hands and feet to alleviate liver issues; Dang Gui (angelica) washed in wine, entering the Shao Yin of the feet to store blood; Fang Feng (siler) and Shan Zhi Ren (gardenia fruit) to purge heart fire, all in equal measures.

    These ingredients are ground into a powder, then combined with honey to form pills the size of a water chestnut. Each dose is half a pill, cooked in bamboo leaf broth with added rock sugar. Xue Ji states: According to the previous formula for the Shao Yin liver channel, it disperses muscle evils and alleviates the internal suffering caused by cold medicines. If there is constipation with thirst for cold drinks, and regular appetite, it pertains to physical illness with excess; thus, this should be used to purge. If the bowel movements are normal with thirst for cold drinks, and the illness stems from excessive Qi causing physical weakness, it is advisable to use 'Yin Qing Wan' to pacify."[137p]
    If the stools are not firm, thirst arises, and drinking soup leads to little food intake and bodily fatigue, indicating both a deficiency of form and illness, it is advisable to use Dihuang Wan to nourish. If there is blood deficiency in the liver channel resulting in wind, first use Siwu Decoction with added Jiao Teng to nourish liver blood, followed by Si Junzi Decoction to support the spleen. If phlegm arises from dryness in the liver channel due to blood deficiency, use Dihuang Wan to generate kidney water and benefit liver blood. If earth cannot nourish wood, use Si Junzi Decoction with added Dang Gui to strengthen the middle, and Dihuang Wan to nourish the kidneys. If the lung metal overcomes the liver wood, use Liu Junzi Decoction with added Shao Yao and Mu Xiang to strengthen the spleen earth to balance the lung metal. If due to heart deficiency it seizes the mother's energy, or if kidney water is deficient and cannot nourish liver wood, Dihuang Wan should be the principal treatment. If strong medicinal agents have been frequently administered, and there is deficiency and cold in the spleen and stomach, Liu Junzi Decoction with added Clove and Mu Xiang must be used to support the spleen and stomach, nourishing the yang energy. If the lactating mother is angry and liver fire is recklessly moving, causing illness to the child, the mother should take Jiawei Xiao Chai Hu Tang. If the lactating mother has deficient liver and spleen blood leading to heat, resulting in illness for the child, the mother should take Jiawei Xiaoyao San, and the child should also take a few drops.[138p]
    Dao Chi San: Treats children's small intestine excess heat and red, painful urination. The main ingredients are the heart of raw Rehmannia and herbs for the small intestine with honey-fried licorice, which is used to clear heart fire. Muxiang helps clear the heat of the small intestine, thus Qian used it to guide out this heat. Mix these ingredients evenly into a powder. Each dose is one qian, dissolved in water with cool bamboo leaves and taken as a decoction. Xue Ji states: "According to Xie Xin Tang and Dao Chi San, they are both remedies for heart and small intestine excess fire." Indeed, the heart is the mother of the spleen, and when the heart is ill, the spleen's energy becomes deficient. Therefore, users should be cautious.

    Mi Zhi An Shen Wan: Treats children's heart blood deficiency, leading to nighttime startle and fright or disturbances from being scared. Ginseng nourishes the five organs and treats fright, while Ban Xia is soaked to dry the spleen and strengthen the stomach. Sour jujube seeds calm the heart and mind. Poria mushrooms enhance mind and spirit, with each ingredient at one qian. Angelica, tangerine peel, and cooked red peony, all at seven parts each, and five grains of Schisandra, pounded together. Honey-fried licorice, three parts. Mix these into a powder and form into pills the size of Job's tears using ginger juice. Take one pill with fresh ginger soup.[139p]
    Xie Huang San treats children with excess heat in the spleen and stomach.

    Ingredients:

    • Fang Feng (2 taels)
    • Huo Xiang (entering the Hand and Foot Taiyin channels) aids the spleen, opens appetite, and stops vomiting (7.5 qian each of both)
    • Sheng Gan Cao (7.5 qian)
    • Shi Gao (5 qian, crushed) to clear stomach fire
    • Shan Zhi Ren (1 liang), treats heat in the stomach

    These are prepared with honey wine, lightly stir-fried into a powder. Take 1-2 qian each time, decocted in water.

    According to Xue Ji, if there is thirst accompanied by a desire to drink cold, the eyes covered while lying down, hands and feet excessively hot, or the skin is yellow all over, this indicates excess heat in the stomach channel, and Xie Huang San should be used.

    If there is thirst and a desire to drink soup, while lying down the eyes are exposed, and hands and feet are cold, this indicates deficient heat in the stomach channel, and Yi Gong San should be used.

    If the face is green, with spasm, a lack of appetite for dairy, this indicates the liver overacting on the spleen, and Mi Zhi Bu Pi Tang should be used.

    If the face is red with agitation, body heat with drowsiness, this indicates the heart overacting on the spleen, and Mi Zhi An Shen Wan should be used.

    If the face is pale with wheezing and coughing, limbs are fatigued, this indicates the lung overacting on the spleen, and Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang should be used.

    If the lips are black with diarrhea, hands and feet are cold, this indicates the kidney overacting on the spleen, and Yi Huang San should be used.

    In cases of insufficient body fluids post-illness, leading to dry mouth and thirst, Qi Wei Bai Zhu San should be used.

    If a nursing mother consumes rich and thick flavors leading to emotional distress and heat, the cause should be evaluated, and the treatment should focus on her.[140p]
    Wumwei Yigong Powder: Treats weak spleen and stomach in children, with symptoms of vomiting, diarrhea, and loss of appetite. Ingredients: Ginseng, Poria, fried Atractylodes, fried Licorice, and aged Tangerine Peel, equal parts. Prepare as a powder, take three qian mixed with ginger and jujube water. Xue Ji states: According to previous prescriptions, this is a holy medicine to tonify the spleen and stomach. Moreover, in the human body, the spleen and stomach are paramount. If children overindulge in milk and food, or if they are exposed to cold conditions, or if their nursing mothers are affected by the six excesses or seven emotions, they drink the mother's milk and various ailments arise, this medicine should be used specifically, and its effects will be as responsive as an echo.

    Yihuang Powder: Treats children with deficiency and coldness of the spleen earth, leading to vomiting and diarrhea. Ingredients: Two qian of Clove to dispel cold from the spleen and stomach; five qian each of Green Tangerine Peel (remove the white part) and Myrobalan fruit pulp, which can open the appetite, aid digestion, and stop diarrhea; and three qian of fried Licorice. Prepare as a powder, take one or two qian boiled in water. Xue Ji states: According to previous symptoms, if vomiting and diarrhea are caused by weak spleen and earth, use Wumwei Yigong Powder. If it is due to the liver wood overpowering the spleen earth, causing vomiting and diarrhea, use Liu Junzi Powder with Chaihu. If there is no response, or if the hands and feet are cold, it is due to spleen and stomach deficiency and cold—add Wood Fragrance and roasted Ginger. If the nursing mother has weak spleen earth, allowing the liver wood to dominate, also treat with previous medicines. If the nursing mother is upset, causing the child to suffer from the above symptoms, she should take a modified Guipi Decoction.[141p]
    Seven Herb Bai Zhu Powder treats children's vomiting and diarrhea, or conditions resulting from insufficient fluids post-illness, manifesting as dry mouth and thirst. Ingredients include: 2 qian of dried kudzu, which can promote stomach qi upward, generate fluids, and is a Yangming channel herb; 1 qian each of ginseng, white atractylodes (stir-fried), saussurea, white poria, honey-fried licorice, and patchouli leaves. The mixture is ground into a fine powder; each dose is 2-3 qian, decocted in water. Xue Ji states: stomach injury causes vomiting, spleen injury causes diarrhea. Thus, this formula adjusts and tonifies the stomach qi to generate fluids. If there are no other symptoms and the only issue is weak stomach qi and insufficient fluids, the Four Gentlemen Decoction is particularly effective. Zhang Jingyue remarks: this formula treats children's symptoms of deficiency heat and thirst. If there are no symptoms of qi stagnation, vomiting, or diarrhea, then reduce or omit saussurea and patchouli to avoid dryness and qi depletion.

    Ejiao Powder treats children's lung deficiency cough and dry mouth. It consists of: 1 liang of Ejiao, stir-fried, which nourishes qi; 1 qian of honey-fried licorice; 5 qian of Aristolochia, which addresses lung heat cough and nourishes the lungs; 1 liang of glutinous rice; 7 blanched apricot kernels, which relieve coughing and regulate qi; and 2.5 qian of mouse glue. The mixture is ground into a fine powder; each dose is 2 qian, decocted in water. Xue Ji notes: if the previous symptoms are due to weakness of the spleen and stomach leading to lung qi deficiency, then Supplementing the Middle and Benefiting Qi Decoction is recommended. If the lung elemental metal is deficient, then the Four Gentlemen Decoction is advisable.[142p]
    Dihuang Pill is used to treat children's kidney deficiency leading to symptoms such as delayed movement and speech.

    The ingredients include:

    • Eight qian of raw rehmannia, washed in wine, to nourish kidney water and replenish true yin, a vital medicine for blood deficiency.
    • Four qian each of shan zhu yu (cornelian cherry) to replenish the kidney and enhance essence, and dried wild yam, which is cool and can nourish.
    • Three qian each of peony bark to cool the kidneys and drain fire within, used to treat bone steaming from deficiency in the foot’s lesser yin without sweating.
    • Ze xie to nourish yin and enter the kidney channel, and bai fu ling.

    These are ground into a paste with raw rehmannia, the remainder is made into powder, and then mixed with processed honey to form pills the size of a tung seed. Each dose is two to thirty pills, taken with warm water on an empty stomach.

    Xue Ji states: This pill treats symptoms of kidney channel deficiency heat with thirst, pale urination and blockage, phlegm and qi stagnation; or liver channel blood deficiency causing dryness and heat, wind affecting excessive energy and leading to scrofula or tuberculosis; or limb spasms and eye twitching; or lung channel deficiency fire causing cough with blood phlegm, and dizziness; or dry and painful throat with sores on the tongue and mouth; or heart blood deficiency with fire causing spontaneous sweating, night sweats, and blood in the stool; or spleen deficiency with damp heat harming the kidney and causing lower back and knee discomfort; or conditions like scabies, eczema, and other ailments, with the aforementioned herbs being the primary treatment, supported by additional medicines for each organ.

    In general, this medicine nourishes water from the heavenly source. If a child has insufficient constitution, is weak and frail, unable to speak properly, fears bright light and has a sinking spirit, experiences five delays and five weaknesses, and has kidney or liver deficiency, or is exposed too early to sexual matters causing depletion of essence and energy harming all organs, this formula is used to nourish and restore, with its benefits too numerous to detail.[143p]
    Si Jun Zi Decoction: Used to treat children with weak spleen Qi, poor digestion, intestinal noises, diarrhea, or vomiting. Ingredients: 5 grams each of ginseng, white poria, white atractylodes, and honey-fried licorice. Prepare by decocting in water.

    Si Wu Decoction: Used to treat children with liver and blood deficiency, fever, worsening in the afternoon, or restlessness and insomnia. Ingredients: 6 grams each of angelica (dong quai) and cooked rehmannia, 3 grams of white peony, and 2.5 grams of chuanxiong. Prepare two doses by decocting in water.

    Ba Zhen Decoction: Used to treat children with both Qi and blood deficiency, which may result from excessive blood loss or depletion of original Qi, leading to internal heat and emaciation. This is a combination of Si Wu and Si Jun Zi Decoctions.

    Shi Quan Da Bu Decoction: Used to treat children with Qi and blood deficiency with heat, which may occur after illness with alternating chills and fever, or symptoms of spontaneous sweating, night sweats, reduced appetite, fatigue, fever and thirst, headaches, and dizziness. This is an addition to Ba Zhen Decoction with astragalus and cinnamon.[144p]
    Liujunzi Decoction is used to treat children with weak spleen and stomach, showing thin bodies and yellow faces; or those suffering from prolonged malaria or dysentery, lacking appetite; or experiencing vomiting and diarrhea, with undigested food; or occasionally suffering from food stagnation; or when the mother has past symptoms that affect the child. Ingredients: 2 qian each of ginseng, white atractylodes, and poria; 1 qian each of dried tangerine peel, pinellia, and honey-fried licorice. Preparation: Take 2-3 qian, decoct in ginger and jujube water.

    Buzhong Yiqi Decoction is used to treat children with insufficient middle qi, feeling drowsy and having fever; or those with weak vital energy, experiencing colds and wind-cold symptoms; or when the nursing mother is overworked and has fever, affecting the child. Ingredients: 5 fen each of honey-fried astragalus, ginseng, lightly toasted white atractylodes, and honey-fried licorice; 5 fen of angelica and dried tangerine peel; 2 fen each of koken (i.e., bulle) and bupleurum. Preparation: Decoct in ginger and jujube water.

    Liuhua Powder is used to treat children with heat toxin causing mouth sores. Ingredients: Equal parts of honey-fried yellow bile, calamus pollen, true indigo, and calcined human placenta. Preparation: Grind into a fine powder and apply.

    Baohe Pill is used to treat food retention in children. Ingredients: 2 liang of hawthorn, 2 liang of fermented sorghum, 1 liang each of pinellia and poria; 5 qian each of dried tangerine peel, forsythia, and radish seeds. Preparation: Grind into a powder, mix into dough-sized round balls, and take 10-20 pills with warm water. Another formula includes 1 liang of fried barley and 5 qian of coptis.[145p]
    Xingxing San: Treats children with acute febrile disease, wind-heat, cough with phlegm obstruction. Ingredients: equal parts of Platycodon, Asarum, Ginseng, honey-fried Licorice, Poria, stir-fried Atractylodes, and Trichosanthes root. Grind into a powder, take two qian each time, adding five leaves of mint, and decoct in water.

    Jia Wei Xiao Yao San: Treats nursing mothers with weak liver and spleen qi and blood, fever, which affects the child. Ingredients: 1 qian each of Angelica, Atractylodes, Poria, and fried Peony; 5 fen each of Bupleurum, fried Moutan root, fried Gardenia, and fried Licorice. Decoct in water to take. Remove Moutan root and Gardenia from this formula to make Xiao Yao San.

    Yin Chen Tang: Treats children with body heat, dry nose and sweating, red and painful urination and defecation, damp-heat causing jaundice. Ingredients: 6 qian of Yin Chen, 2 Gardenias, and 2 qian of Rhubarb. Take 1 qian each time, decoct in water.

    Da Lian Qiao Yin: Treats children with accumulated heat causing difficulty in urination and defecation; or lingering toxins after measles, leading to sores on limbs; or nodules that wander and persist. Ingredients: 1 qian each of Forsythia, Qumai, Schizonepeta, Mutong, red Peony, Angelica, Fangfeng, Bupleurum, Talcum, Cicada slough, and Licorice; 5 fen each of stir-fried Gardenia and stir-fried Huangqin. Take 1 to 2 qian each time, decoct in water.[146p]
    Niu Huang Pill treats children's fever and dissipates accumulation.

    Prepare 1 qian of realgar, water ground; 1 qian of Qian Niu; 2 qian of Tian Zhu Huang. Mix these into a powder, roll into pills the size of millet. Take 5 to 7 pills with mint water.

    Bai Bing Zi treats children with abdominal masses, who drink milk, cough, and vomit phlegm.

    Use 1 qian each of talc and light powder, half a summer's decoction soaked and roasted, and Nan Xing. Add 24 peeled and membrane-removed Yuan beans, boil in 1 liter of water until dry, then grind into paste. Form into balls the size of mung beans, flatten into cakes. Take 2 to 3 cakes with boiled scallion white soup.

    Xue Ji said: All using this method, including Li Jing Wan, Zi Shuang Wan, San Wei Niu Huang Wan, and Bian Yin Wan, are critical medicines that can overturn life and death. One must carefully consider the physical condition of the illness before administering; fear of mistakes can be fatal! The classics say: where evil gathers, its energy must be weak; if it lingers, the illness is real. If the illness is real, its energy is real and the vitality is weak. Doctor Dong Yuan states: when both the form and illness are real, one should drain, not tonify; when both are weak, one should tonify, not drain. Those treating must discern! Zhang Jingyue said: sometimes use Zi Su decoction. Avoid hot substances. Adjust dosage for children.[147p]
    Cool Shock Pill treats children's sudden fright and malnutrition.

    Ingredients:

    • Three qian of fried Gentian Root
    • Three qian of Fangfeng (Siler)
    • Three qian of Indigo
    • Two qian of Diao Teng Gou (Jiaogulan)
    • One qian and two fen of fried Huang Lian (Coptis)
    • One qian of Dragon Brain (a fragrant resin)
    • Two fen each of Niu Huang (Cattle Gall) and Musk

    These are ground into a powder, mixed with a paste to form pills the size of millet. Take ten pills with a decoction of honeysuckle.
